news_summary: Today's human rights progress highlights support for working parents, the global impact of courageous journalism, and efforts to create safer online environments for children.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées enregistrées aujourd'hui en matière de droits de l'homme mettent en avant le soutien apporté aux parents qui travaillent, l'impact mondial d'un journalisme courageux et les efforts visant à créer des environnements en ligne plus sûrs pour les enfants.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve el apoyo a los padres que trabajan, el impacto mundial del periodismo valiente y los esfuerzos por crear entornos en línea más seguros para los niños.

articles.title: What nobody mentions in the return-to-office debate: babies
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Governments have spent decades and billions trying to reverse falling birth rates. Cash bonuses for new parents in South Korea. Generous parental leave in Scandinavia. Subsidized childcare across the EU. And yet fertility rates in most high-income countries keep sliding. A major new study suggests one of the most […]
The post What nobody mentions in the return-to-office debate: babies first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Governments have spent decades and billions trying to reverse falling birth rates. Cash bonuses for new parents in South Korea. Generous parental leave in Scandinavia. Subsidized childcare across the EU. And yet fertility rates in most high-income countries keep sliding. A major new study suggests one of the most effective levers has been operating in the background since 2020, and it costs governments almost nothing to maintain.

Remote work’s unexpected demographic effect

Research co-authored by Dr. Cevat Giray Aksoy of King’s College London, drawing on data from nearly 40 countries, finds a direct link between remote work and fertility. When both partners work from home at least one day a week, lifetime fertility increases by an average of 0.32 children per woman compared to couples where neither partner works remotely. The number sounds modest. Scale it up, and it isn’t.

In the US, current remote work levels account for roughly 8.1 percent of births in 2024. That’s approximately 291,000 babies. In England, the figure is around 6.2 percent of births, or about 35,400. Compared to a world where remote work never expanded beyond pre-pandemic levels, the UK likely saw about 41,000 additional births in 2024 alone.

Why flexibility is doing what cash bonuses couldn’t

Remote work reduces what researchers call the “time and coordination costs” of combining a career with raising children. No commute means more time at home. Flexible hours make it easier to handle school pickups, sick days, and the general unpredictability of family life. For a lot of couples, the barrier to having children was never desire; it was logistics.

The UK offers a useful data point. With 54 percent of university-educated adult workers now working from home at least one day a week, it has one of the highest remote work rates in the world. The study’s authors say this is having a measurable effect on British demographics, and the numbers back them up.

The countries with the most to gain

The findings carry particular weight for nations where flexible work has remained limited. Japan and South Korea, both deep into demographic contractions, have historically kept remote work rare. The researchers calculate that if either country adopted flexible work at UK levels, national fertility rates could rise by more than four percent. For economies already dealing with shrinking workforces and aging populations, that is a serious number.

The question nobody is asking about return to office

This research lands at an uncomfortable moment. Many large employers have spent the past two years pushing workers back to the office, citing productivity, culture, and collaboration. Some governments have echoed that pressure. But if this data holds, policies that reduce remote work access may be working against demographic recovery, a trade-off that has received almost no public attention.

Dr. Aksoy was direct about both what remote work can and cannot do on its own: “Remote work will not reverse decades of demographic decline on its own. But in a world where conventional pro-natalist policies are expensive and often disappoint, flexibility over where we work is emerging as one of the most promising and cheapest ways to help people have the families they say they want.”

People in most of these countries already want larger families. The obstacle has been structural, not motivational. Remote work does not solve every piece of that puzzle, but evidence from nearly 40 countries now suggests it is solving more of it than anyone expected when laptops first got carried home in 2020.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant new study showing that remote work, a flexible work policy, has a measurable positive impact on fertility rates in high-income countries. This represents a meaningful societal benefit by addressing demographic decline, a major challenge affecting many nations. The article provides detailed data and context, highlighting the broad significance for population growth and family life, and suggesting a low-cost, scalable policy lever that could improve quality of life and societal sustainability.
articles.personality_title: Remote work helps more babies be born in wealthy countries, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many rich countries have been worried about falling birth rates for years. Governments have tried things like giving money to new parents and offering paid leave, but birth rates keep dropping.

**What happened** – A new study looked at data from almost 40 countries and found that when both parents work from home at least one day a week, they tend to have more children. On average, women have about 0.32 more children than couples who don’t work remotely. In the US, this means around 291,000 extra babies born in 2024 because of remote work. In England, it’s about 35,400 more babies.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows that flexible work helps parents manage time better, making it easier to have children. Unlike expensive government programs, letting people work from home costs very little and still encourages families to grow. Countries like the UK, where many people work remotely, are already seeing this effect. Places like Japan and South Korea, which have fewer remote workers and very low birth rates, could see bigger improvements if they adopt similar work policies.

**What's next step** – As some companies and governments want people back in offices, this study suggests they should think carefully. Reducing remote work might make it harder for families to grow. Policymakers might use this information to support flexible work as a way to help with population challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Allowing parents to work from home even part-time helps more babies be born by making family life easier and more flexible.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le travail à distance favorise la naissance de plus de bébés dans les pays riches, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux pays riches s’inquiètent depuis des années de la baisse du taux de natalité. Les gouvernements ont essayé des mesures comme des primes aux nouveaux parents et des congés payés, mais les naissances continuent de diminuer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude, basée sur des données de près de 40 pays, montre que lorsque les deux parents travaillent au moins un jour par semaine à distance, ils ont tendance à avoir plus d’enfants. En moyenne, les femmes ont environ 0,32 enfant en plus que les couples qui ne travaillent pas à distance. Aux États-Unis, cela représente environ 291 000 naissances supplémentaires en 2024 grâce au télétravail. En Angleterre, ce sont environ 35 400 bébés en plus.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que le travail flexible aide les parents à mieux gérer leur temps, ce qui facilite la venue d’enfants. Contrairement aux programmes gouvernementaux coûteux, permettre le télétravail coûte très peu tout en encourageant la croissance des familles. Des pays comme le Royaume-Uni, où beaucoup de personnes travaillent à distance, observent déjà cet effet. Des pays comme le Japon et la Corée du Sud, avec peu de télétravail et des taux de natalité très bas, pourraient voir de plus grandes améliorations en adoptant des politiques similaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Alors que certaines entreprises et gouvernements veulent que les gens reviennent au bureau, cette étude suggère de réfléchir à deux fois. Réduire le télétravail pourrait compliquer la croissance des familles. Les décideurs pourraient utiliser ces informations pour soutenir le travail flexible afin d’aider à relever les défis démographiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Permettre aux parents de travailler à domicile même à temps partiel aide à faire naître plus de bébés en rendant la vie de famille plus facile et flexible.
articles.personality_title_es: El trabajo remoto ayuda a que nazcan más bebés en países ricos, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países ricos han estado preocupados durante años por la baja tasa de natalidad. Los gobiernos han probado cosas como dar dinero a nuevos padres y ofrecer licencias pagadas, pero los nacimientos siguen disminuyendo.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio que analizó datos de casi 40 países encontró que cuando ambos padres trabajan desde casa al menos un día a la semana, tienden a tener más hijos. En promedio, las mujeres tienen alrededor de 0,32 hijos más que las parejas que no trabajan de forma remota. En Estados Unidos, esto significa aproximadamente 291,000 bebés adicionales nacidos en 2024 gracias al trabajo remoto. En Inglaterra, son unos 35,400 bebés más.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que el trabajo flexible ayuda a los padres a manejar mejor su tiempo, facilitando tener hijos. A diferencia de los programas gubernamentales costosos, permitir trabajar desde casa cuesta muy poco y aún así fomenta el crecimiento familiar. Países como el Reino Unido, donde muchas personas trabajan remotamente, ya están viendo este efecto. Lugares como Japón y Corea del Sur, con pocos trabajadores remotos y tasas de natalidad muy bajas, podrían mejorar mucho si adoptan políticas similares.

**Próximo paso** – Mientras algunas empresas y gobiernos presionan para que la gente regrese a las oficinas, este estudio sugiere que deberían pensarlo bien. Reducir el trabajo remoto podría dificultar que las familias crezcan. Los responsables de políticas podrían usar esta información para apoyar el trabajo flexible y ayudar con los desafíos demográficos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Permitir que los padres trabajen desde casa aunque sea parte del tiempo ayuda a que nazcan más bebés al hacer la vida familiar más fácil y flexible.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy home interior scene featuring two overlapping transparent silhouettes of adults working side-by-side at desks with laptops, connected by flowing gentle lines that transform into small, glowing baby figures cradled within the shared space, all rendered in warm, muted earth tones and soft pastels, capturing the harmony of remote work fostering family growth and new life.

articles.title: How did an Afghan woman journalist’s writing resonate in China?
articles.summary: Afghan journalist Khadija Haidary’s writing unexpectedly resonated with Chinese readers, sparking emotional responses and small acts of cross-border solidarity.
articles.content:
When Afghan journalist Khadija Haidary fled the Taliban, she never imagined that her writing would reach readers thousands of miles away in China. Yet it did — prompting small but meaningful acts of support that empowered her to move forward amid her uncertain situation. In China, where civil society is tightly regulated and spontaneous cross-border humanitarian support is rare, her letters, which evolved into a book titled “A Letter from an Afghan Woman,” sparked an unexpected cross-border solidarity with the oppressed women from far away. Rather than forming a visible movement, these responses took shape as quiet, individual acts, revealing how solidarity adapts under constraint.

The story began in October 2024, when a Chinese journalist, Weilin Hong (洪蔚琳), translated and published her month-long email correspondence with “Haidary on Positive Links” (正面連結), a medium-sized WeChat account covering social issues to enlighten Chinese readers on the transformation of Afghan society under the Taliban. The Islamic militant organization, which ruled the region between 1996 and 2001, recaptured Kabul in 2021, after the U.S. withdrew its troops.

Through emails, Haidary explained how women lost their rights between 2021 and 2024: they were forced out of their jobs and forbidden from walking the streets alone; women were not allowed to be treated by male doctors, even as women were blocked from attending medical schools; girls were barred from schools, parks, and swimming pools, and many were forced to marry before reaching adulthood; journalists were imprisoned and in some cases killed for telling the truth. These accounts are not abstract statistics — they are Haidary’s lived experiences. She lost her job, male friends, social life, and bore witness to the harassment of women every day.

In China, where civil society is limited, and media coverage of foreign crises is selective, such storytelling carries particular weight. Haidary’s personal narrative on gender oppression in Afghanistan quickly went viral online and generated emotional resonances among female Chinese readers. One Chinese reader wrote on Weibo, addressing the subtle connection among women across the border:

人类跨越国界高墙，其本质相似，心灵相通 / 他们想封住女人的嘴，她就成为劈开黑夜的光。

Across borders and walls, human nature remains the same, and hearts are connected / They want women shut up; she becomes the light that cuts through the darkness at night.

Haidary was writing for Zan Times, an Afghan women’s media outlet, and taking refuge in the countryside to avoid the Taliban when the Chinese journalist first reached out to her in September 2024. The email exchanges encouraged Haidary to consider leaving the country:

我第一次清晰地感受到：我的故事、我的斗争、我的苦难，是重要的；我必须找到一个能够自由说话的地方，去讲述发生在我们身上的一切。你知道吗，洪蔚琳的邮件让我意识到，世界上确实有人在关心我们的痛苦。我知道自己必须鼓起全部勇气，尽一切可能离开这里。

For the first time, I realized that my story, my struggles, and my suffering matter; I had to find a place where I could speak freely and tell everything that had happened to us. You know, Hong Weilin’s email made me aware that there are people in the world who care about our pain. I knew I had to muster all my courage and do everything in my power to get out of here.

By early October 2024, Haidary and her family were settled in Pakistan. As her letters gained public attention, a Chinese publisher reached out to offer her a publishing contract and a royalties advance, which could help her family end their status as exiles in Pakistan and resettle in Canada.

The book was eventually published in August 2025, and within months, more than 10,000 copies were sold. Knowing that the book's royalties would help Haidary’s family resettle in a less precarious environment, many Chinese have helped promote her book through reviews and reflections on social media.

In one widely circulated post on the Instagram-like Xiaohongshu, the user “WOMEN’s view on the world” (WOMEN看世界) contrasted historical images of empowered, free Afghan women with today’s restrictions, accompanied by the following caption: “They, too, once lived vivid and vibrant lives.” The main text of this post says:

翻开这本书，倾听最真实的声音，以18篇短篇小说，揭开最伤痛的回忆 / 跨越过界与偏见，看见本身，就是一种力量。

Opening the book, readers encounter some of the most authentic voices, as 18 short stories unveil deeply painful memories / To look beyond borders and prejudice, and to truly see people as they are, is in itself a form of strength.

In this sense, Afghan women’s stories function not only as distant narratives but as a mirror through which Chinese readers negotiate their own unarticulated experiences. Some readers have drawn parallels between Afghan women’s experiences and ongoing gender-related conversations in China, particularly around restrictions on personal autonomy, social expectations, and the shrinking space for feminist expression. These reflections can be seen in discussions among overseas Chinese students and feminist communities on platforms such as WeChat and Telegram, where participants related Haidary’s accounts to their own experiences.

These perspectives are also reflected, though less explicitly, in mainstream media discourse. A book review published in the state-sponsored outlet Beijing Daily noted that Haidary’s story is not only read as a distant narrative of suffering but also interpreted through readers’ own social realities:

尽管故事发生在遥远的阿富汗，但其中所蕴含的恐惧、挣扎，以及对家庭的牵挂，是人类共通的情感。它提醒着我们，和平与自由并非与生俱来，而是需要珍惜与守护。

Although the stories take place in Afghanistan, the fear, the struggles, and the family ties are universal sentiments. It [the book] reminds us that peace and freedom is not given, but something that we have to treasure and safeguard.

Some also lauded the resilience of Afghan women living in an oppressive environment. One user left the following comment on a book review on Douban, a Chinese platform for user-generated reviews of books, films, and cultural content:

即便在这样的压力之下，阿迪亚依然写出了独属于阿富汗女性的坚韧、热情和对自由的渴望。书中描绘的女性并非被动的受害者：有的加入国民军找回自尊，有的在绝望中寻找巫师抗争，有的在流言蜚语中坚持自我。正如哈迪亚在自序中所写：“除了战争和破坏之外，人们还必须有其他东西来介绍自己。

Even under such pressure, Haidary managed to capture the resilience, passion, and yearning for freedom that are unique to Afghan women. The women portrayed in the book are not passive victims: some joined the National Army to reclaim their dignity, some seek out shamans in their desperation to fight back, and some remain true to themselves amid rumors and gossip. As Haidary writes in her preface: ‘Beyond war and destruction, people must have something else to define themselves.’

In addition to online promotion, some readers took further steps to support her. One reader even delivered the book to Haidary during a business trip to Pakistan in November 2025.

In such a constrained environment, solidarity does not disappear; it becomes quieter, more fragmented, and often deeply personal.

All these small acts of feminist solidarity were carried out without widespread mobilization or calls to action, as feminist social media outlets have been banned and related networks repressed in recent years. Citizen-initiated fundraising and advocacy work is highly sensitive and almost impossible under China’s tightly regulated online environment. Hong highlighted the cross-border solidarity among women in a reflective piece in January 2026:

在如今人人默认全球趋向保守排外的时代，这样动人的、毫无私心的援助像接力棒一样发生在中国，并奇迹般地帮到了一个他国女性。

As the world is becoming more conservative and xenophobic, this touching and selfless act of kindness has spread like a relay across China, and miraculously helped a woman from another country.

Indeed, the willingness to learn and understand others’ suffering is precious in today’s chaotic world, as explained in a post on Sohu:

哈迪亚·海达里的作品，既是一面镜子，也是一把钥匙。它映照出阿富汗女性的苦难，也叩问着每一个读者的责任：当世界在喧嚣中遗忘某些角落的伤痛时，我们是否愿意成为“知道”的人？这本书的答案或许不在于提供解决方案，而在于唤醒共情。正如书中所写：“她们并不遥远。”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful cross-border solidarity sparked by an Afghan woman journalist's writing, which raised awareness of women's oppression under the Taliban and inspired support from Chinese readers despite restrictive conditions. This story highlights the power of personal narrative to foster empathy and support for human rights, with tangible impact including a published book that aids the journalist's family resettlement. The scope is broad as it connects struggles of women across countries and cultures, and the article provides substantial context and details.
articles.personality_title: Afghan journalist’s story inspires quiet support from Chinese readers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
After the Taliban took control of Afghanistan in 2021, many women lost basic rights like going to school or working. Khadija Haidary, an Afghan journalist, lived through these hardships and shared her experiences through writing.

**What happened**
In 2024, a Chinese journalist translated Haidary’s emails about life under the Taliban and shared them online. These writings touched many Chinese readers, especially women, who responded with small acts of support like promoting her book and sending messages of encouragement. Haidary eventually left Afghanistan, settled in Pakistan, and published a book in China that sold over 10,000 copies.

**Impact**
This story is special because it shows how people in very different countries can connect and support each other even when big public movements are not possible. Haidary’s personal stories helped Chinese readers understand women’s struggles in Afghanistan and reflect on their own challenges. The support she received also gave her family hope for a safer future.

**What’s next step**
With the help from book sales and readers’ support, Haidary and her family plan to move to Canada for a more stable life. The story also encourages more sharing of personal stories across borders, helping build understanding in places where open activism is difficult.

**One-sentence takeaway**
A journalist’s honest stories about life under the Taliban built a quiet bridge of empathy and support between Afghan and Chinese women.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’histoire d’une journaliste afghane suscite un soutien discret parmi les lecteurs chinois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Après la prise de pouvoir des talibans en Afghanistan en 2021, de nombreuses femmes ont perdu des droits essentiels comme aller à l’école ou travailler. Khadija Haidary, une journaliste afghane, a vécu ces difficultés et les a partagées par écrit.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
En 2024, un journaliste chinois a traduit les courriels de Haidary décrivant la vie sous les talibans et les a publiés en ligne. Ces écrits ont touché de nombreux lecteurs chinois, surtout des femmes, qui ont répondu par de petits gestes de soutien comme promouvoir son livre et envoyer des messages d’encouragement. Haidary a finalement quitté l’Afghanistan, s’est installée au Pakistan et a publié un livre en Chine qui s’est vendu à plus de 10 000 exemplaires.

**Impact**
Cette histoire est unique car elle montre comment des personnes de pays très différents peuvent se connecter et s’entraider même lorsque les grands mouvements publics sont impossibles. Les récits personnels de Haidary ont aidé les lecteurs chinois à comprendre les luttes des femmes afghanes et à réfléchir sur leurs propres défis. Le soutien reçu a aussi donné de l’espoir à sa famille pour un avenir plus sûr.

**Prochaine étape**
Grâce aux ventes du livre et au soutien des lecteurs, Haidary et sa famille prévoient de s’installer au Canada pour une vie plus stable. Cette histoire encourage aussi le partage d’histoires personnelles à travers les frontières, aidant à construire la compréhension dans des endroits où l’activisme ouvert est difficile.

**En une phrase**
Les récits sincères d’une journaliste sur la vie sous les talibans ont créé un pont discret d’empathie et de soutien entre les femmes afghanes et chinoises.
articles.personality_title_es: La historia de una periodista afgana inspira un apoyo silencioso entre lectores chinos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Después de que los talibanes tomaron el control de Afganistán en 2021, muchas mujeres perdieron derechos básicos como ir a la escuela o trabajar. Khadija Haidary, una periodista afgana, vivió estas dificultades y las compartió a través de sus escritos.

**Qué pasó**
En 2024, un periodista chino tradujo los correos electrónicos de Haidary que describían la vida bajo los talibanes y los publicó en línea. Estos relatos conmovieron a muchos lectores chinos, especialmente mujeres, que respondieron con pequeños actos de apoyo como promover su libro y enviar mensajes de ánimo. Haidary finalmente dejó Afganistán, se estableció en Pakistán y publicó un libro en China que vendió más de 10,000 copias.

**Impacto**
Esta historia es especial porque muestra cómo personas de países muy distintos pueden conectarse y apoyarse, incluso cuando no es posible un gran movimiento público. Los relatos personales de Haidary ayudaron a los lectores chinos a entender las luchas de las mujeres afganas y a reflexionar sobre sus propios desafíos. El apoyo recibido también dio esperanza a su familia para un futuro más seguro.

**Próximo paso**
Con la ayuda de las ventas del libro y el apoyo de los lectores, Haidary y su familia planean mudarse a Canadá para tener una vida más estable. La historia también fomenta compartir relatos personales a través de las fronteras, ayudando a crear comprensión en lugares donde el activismo abierto es difícil.

**Una frase clave**
Las historias sinceras de una periodista sobre la vida bajo los talibanes construyeron un puente silencioso de empatía y apoyo entre mujeres afganas y chinas.
articles.image_prompt: A softly lit, detailed painting of two intertwined paper lanterns—one decorated with delicate Afghan patterns and the other with subtle Chinese motifs—glowing warmly against a muted background, connected by a thread of handwritten letters floating gently between them, symbolizing cross-border solidarity, resilience, and shared stories of women overcoming oppression.

articles.title: Towards a safer online space for kids: interview with European Commission Executive Vice-President Henna Virkkunen
articles.summary: On Thursday, March 26, the European Newsroom sat down with Virkkunen for an interview focused on the EU’s efforts to protect minors in the online environment – from social media to adult websites.
articles.content:
On Thursday, March 26, the European Newsroom sat down with Virkkunen for an interview focused on the EU’s efforts to protect minors in the online environment – from social media to adult websites.

Across every corner of the internet, EU kids deserve a safe digital playground. That was the message of Henna Virkkunen, European Commission Executive Vice-President for Tech Sovereignty, Security and Democracy during an interview with the European Newsroom (enr) in Brussels focusing on the protection of minors online.

The European Commission has taken aim at some of the world’s biggest adult websites, announcing preliminary findings that Pornhub, Stripchat, XNXX and XVideos breach the EU’s landmark Digital Services Act (DSA). After opening proceedings in May last year, Brussels now says the platforms are failing to shield minors from explicit content, pointing in particular to weak or ineffective age verification systems that leave underage users exposed.

The Commission also turned its attention to Snapchat, opening an investigation to determine whether the social media platform is complying with the DSA. The probe comes amid concerns that minors could be exposed to serious risks on the app, including grooming and the illegal sale of drugs, vapes, and alcohol.

Journalists from European Newsroom partner agencies ANSA (Italy), CTK (Czech Republic), dpa (Germany), EFE (Spain), TT (Sweden), and other media were given an exclusive first look, getting the chance to quiz Virkkunen directly about her latest announcements on child safety and online platform regulation under the DSA.

In addition, journalists also asked questions about the EU’s age verification app – which is currently in a testing phase – as well as broader issues including addictive platform design, the responsibility of social media companies to protect minors, and the proposed “chat control” measures, which have raised significant privacy concerns across Europe.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the European Commission's concrete regulatory actions and investigations aimed at protecting minors from harmful online content and risks, demonstrating a significant and broad societal effort to improve child safety on the internet. The focus on enforcement of the Digital Services Act and development of age verification technology shows substantive progress in safeguarding children's rights and wellbeing online.
articles.personality_title: EU steps up to protect children from harmful online content
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The internet offers many places where children can spend time, but some websites and apps have content that is not safe for them. The European Union (EU) wants to make sure kids are protected when they go online.

**What happened** – On March 26, Henna Virkkunen, a top EU official, spoke about efforts to keep children safe on the internet. The EU is investigating big adult websites like Pornhub and Snapchat because they may not be doing enough to stop children from seeing harmful content. The EU also has a new age verification app being tested to help check if users are old enough to enter certain sites.

**Impact** – This is important because children can accidentally see harmful or dangerous things online. The EU’s actions show that rules like the Digital Services Act (DSA) are being used to make websites safer. By focusing on age checks and investigating risky platforms, the EU is working to reduce dangers like exposure to adult content and illegal activities.

**What's next step** – The investigations will continue, and the EU will decide if these websites need to change their rules or face penalties. The age verification app will be tested more to improve how it works. These steps could lead to safer internet rules for children across Europe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU is actively enforcing new laws and testing tools to better protect children from harmful online content and risks.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’UE renforce la protection des enfants contre les contenus dangereux en ligne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Internet offre de nombreux espaces où les enfants peuvent passer du temps, mais certains sites et applications contiennent des contenus inadaptés. L’Union européenne (UE) souhaite protéger les enfants lorsqu’ils naviguent en ligne.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 26 mars, Henna Virkkunen, une haute responsable de l’UE, a parlé des efforts pour assurer la sécurité des enfants sur internet. L’UE enquête sur de grands sites pour adultes comme Pornhub et Snapchat, car ils ne protègeraient pas assez les mineurs. L’UE teste aussi une nouvelle application de vérification de l’âge pour vérifier si les utilisateurs sont assez âgés.

**Impact** – C’est important parce que les enfants peuvent voir par erreur des contenus dangereux. Les actions de l’UE montrent que la loi sur les services numériques (DSA) est appliquée pour rendre les sites plus sûrs. En se concentrant sur la vérification de l’âge et en enquêtant sur les plateformes à risque, l’UE réduit les dangers liés à l’exposition à des contenus pour adultes et à des activités illégales.

**Prochaine étape** – Les enquêtes vont continuer et l’UE décidera si ces sites doivent changer leurs règles ou être sanctionnés. L’application de vérification de l’âge sera testée davantage pour être améliorée. Ces mesures pourraient conduire à des règles internet plus sûres pour les enfants en Europe.

**En une phrase** – L’UE applique activement de nouvelles lois et teste des outils pour mieux protéger les enfants des contenus et risques dangereux en ligne.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE intensifica la protección de los niños contra contenidos peligrosos en línea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Internet ofrece muchos lugares donde los niños pueden pasar tiempo, pero algunos sitios y aplicaciones tienen contenido que no es seguro para ellos. La Unión Europea (UE) quiere asegurar que los niños estén protegidos cuando usan internet.

**Qué pasó** – El 26 de marzo, Henna Virkkunen, una alta funcionaria de la UE, habló sobre los esfuerzos para mantener seguros a los niños en internet. La UE está investigando grandes sitios para adultos como Pornhub y Snapchat porque podrían no estar haciendo lo suficiente para evitar que los niños vean contenido dañino. La UE también está probando una nueva aplicación para verificar la edad y asegurarse de que los usuarios tengan la edad adecuada para entrar a ciertos sitios.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque los niños pueden ver por accidente cosas peligrosas en internet. Las acciones de la UE muestran que están usando leyes como la Ley de Servicios Digitales (DSA) para hacer los sitios más seguros. Al enfocarse en verificar la edad y en investigar plataformas riesgosas, la UE trabaja para reducir peligros como la exposición a contenido adulto y actividades ilegales.

**Próximo paso** – Las investigaciones continuarán y la UE decidirá si estos sitios deben cambiar sus reglas o enfrentar sanciones. La aplicación para verificar la edad será probada más para mejorar su funcionamiento. Estos pasos podrían llevar a reglas de internet más seguras para los niños en toda Europa.

**Resumen en una frase** – La UE está aplicando activamente nuevas leyes y probando herramientas para proteger mejor a los niños de contenidos y riesgos peligrosos en línea.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, sturdy digital shield glowing softly in gentle blues and greens, protecting a group of playful, stylized child-like silhouettes made of light, surrounded by abstract representations of social media icons and web elements dimmed and kept at a safe distance, symbolizing the European Commission’s efforts to safeguard minors online through regulation and age verification.
